coredata.dc:title: Carboxyl groups on carbon nanodots as co-reactant sites for anodic electrochemiluminescence of tris(2,2-bipyridine)ruthenium(II)
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Ying
Wang, Zhi-Gang
Qi, Bao-Ping
Liu, Cui
Tang, Bo
Zhang, Zhi-Ling
Liu, Shu-Lin
Pang, Dai-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanodots (C-dots) with good biocompatibility have been extensively utilized as co-reactants for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of the tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) system. However, the ECL intensity of this system is still relatively low and the mechanism of C-dots as co-reactants remains unclear, which greatly limits its further application in bio-analysis. In this work, we revealed that the carboxyl groups on C-dots are co-reactant sites for Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL by systematically investigating the contribution of carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of C-dots to the ECL intensity. Further treatment with hydrogen peroxide to increase the carboxyl-group content on C-dots resulted in a 10-fold increase in ECL intensity over the original Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots system. This work provides new insights for the rational design of ECL systems with C-dots as co-reactants and offers new chances for further applications of C-dots in the field of ECL.

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chemical passivation of the oxygen-containing functional groups on carbon nanodots
Structural characterization of carbon nanodots and their derivatives
Carbon nanodots or their derivatives as co-reactants for Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL
Carboxyl groups on carbon nanodots as co-reactant sites
Proposed mechanism for Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL with carbon nanodots as co-reactants
Enhancing the ECL intensity through surface modification of carbon nanodots

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22293030, 22293032), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFA0210100), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (63211023), and the Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Illustration of the selectively deactivated process for carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups at C-dots, respectively; TEM pictures for (B) C-dots and (C) BA-C-dots. Insets: the corresponding representative picture (left) and size distribution histogram (right); (D) O 1s high-resolution XPS spectra and (E) Zeta potential measurements of C-dots and their derivatives (n = 3).

(A) ECL-potential and (D) CV profiles for Ru(bpy)3 2+ (a) alone and with (b) C-dots, (c) BrAP-C-dots, (d) BA-C-dots, and (e) PH-C-dots; (B) ECL spectrum for Ru(bpy)3 2+/BA-C-dots (n = 3); (C) FL (bottom) and UV–vis absorption (top) spectra for (a) BA-C-dots, (b) Ru(bpy)3 2+, and (c) Ru(bpy)3 2+/BA-C-dots. The excitation wavelength of the FL spectra was 450 nm; (E) The oxidation current of Ru(bpy)3 2+ with different levels of BA-C-dots (n = 3); (F) The pH-dependent intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+ containing C-dots, benzylic alcohol (inset, a) and oxalic acid (inset, b) (n = 3).

(A) Illustration of the simultaneously deactivated process for hydroxyl and carboxyl groups at C-dots; (B) TEM picture for PS-C-dots. Inset: the representative picture (left) and size distribution histogram (right); 1H NMR (C) and FT-IR (D) spectra for C-dots (top) and PS-C-dots (bottom); (E) ECL-potential images for Ru(bpy)3 2+ containing (a) C-dots and (b) PS-C-dots.

(A) TEM picture of ox-C-dots. Inset: the representative picture (left) and size distribution diagram (right); (B) C 1 s XPS and (C) 1H NMR spectra of C-dots (top) and ox-C-dots (bottom); (D) CV and (E) ECL profiles of Ru(bpy)3 2+ with ox-C-dots (a) and C-dots (b); (F) ECL response of Ru(bpy)3 2+/ox-C-dots over 21 cycles.
Schematic illustration of the mechanism for the anodic Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL with C-dots as co-reactants.

head.ce:title: Carboxyl groups on carbon nanodots as co-reactant sites for anodic electrochemiluminescence of tris(2,2-bipyridine)ruthenium(II)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The three typical oxygen-containing functional groups on C-dots have been selectively deactivated to elucidate their impact on the co-reactant behavior of C-dots, and the carboxyl groups on C-dots are revealed for the first time as co-reactant sites for Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL.

Carbon nanodots (C-dots) with good biocompatibility have been extensively utilized as co-reactants for electrochemiluminescence (ECL) of the tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) system. However, the ECL intensity of this system is still relatively low and the mechanism of C-dots as co-reactants remains unclear, which greatly limits its further application in bio-analysis. In this work, we revealed that the carboxyl groups on C-dots are co-reactant sites for Ru(bpy)3 2+ ECL by systematically investigating the contribution of carboxyl, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on the surface of C-dots to the ECL intensity. Further treatment with hydrogen peroxide to increase the carboxyl-group content on C-dots resulted in a 10-fold increase in ECL intensity over the original Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots system. This work provides new insights for the rational design of ECL systems with C-dots as co-reactants and offers new chances for further applications of C-dots in the field of ECL.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is an electrochemical reaction-induced luminescence process [1]. Typically, the ECL signal is generated from a reaction between the luminophore anion and the luminophore cation (the ion annihilation pathway) or a reaction between the luminophore ion and the radical produced by a co-reactant (the co-reactant pathway). In contrast to ion annihilation ECL, co-reactant ECL is capable of being produced by using a single directional voltage scan, thus overcoming the limited potential window of the solvent and facilitating practical applications [2]. Currently, ECL systems involving tripropylamine (TPrA) as a co-reactant and tris(2,2′-bipyridine)ruthenium(II) (Ru(bpy)3 2+) as a luminophore are commonly employed in commercial immunoassays and DNA analysis [3]. However, TPrA has several disadvantages, such as being toxic, volatile, and poorly soluble in water [4]. Therefore, efficient co-reactants with low background, good solubility, and biocompatibility are urgently needed for a wide range of bioanalytical applications [5]. With decades of rapid development, nanomaterials with unique properties have provided new opportunities for ECL [6]. In particular, a series of nanomaterials have been used as co-reactants, including carbon nanomaterials [7–9], nitride nanomaterials [10–12], and II-VI semiconductor nanomaterials [13–15]. It has been reported that nanomaterials as co-reactants can not only promote ECL performance but also develop new sensing strategies [16]. However, the study of nanomaterials as co-reactants is still in its infant stage [17].

Carbon nanodots (C-dots) are a kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with sizes less than 10 nm. They possess an sp2 domain with oxygen-containing functional groups covering the surface [18,19]. As a new type of carbon nanoparticle, C-dots are gradually becoming a rising star by virtue of their small sizes, excellent water solubility, outstanding photophysical properties, and prominent biocompatibility [20,21]. Our group first reported that C-dots can be used as “oxidative-reduction” co-reactants [22]. Recently, ECL systems involving C-dots as co-reactants have been extensively applied in bio-sensing applications [23–26]. Present research on the ECL enhancing mechanism suggests that alcohol or amine groups on C-dots are co-reactant sites [22,27–29]. Our group found the benzylic alcohol groups to be the co-reactant sites of C-dots [22]. The removal of the oxygen-containing groups on C-dots would decrease the ECL intensity, while reducing the C-dots with NaBH4 would increase the ECL intensity. The amino functional groups, in which the α-carbon should contain a hydrogen atom, were found to be the co-reactant sites of C-dots by Carrara et al. [27]. The findings that the ECL intensity is increased tenfold by converting the primary amine of C-dots to a tertiary amine supported the hypothesis. However, to date, the ECL enhancing mechanism is not well understood, and the ECL intensity still has much room to improve because the corresponding research is still scarce. Thus, it is necessary to provide direct and convincing chemical evidence to identify the co-reactant sites on C-dots, especially those unidentified ones, facilitating a rational strategy to improve the ECL intensity for further bioanalytical applications.

Herein, we selectively deactivated the three typical oxygen-containing functional groups of C-dots (Fig. 1 A) to elucidate their impact on the co-reactant behavior of C-dots, respectively. For the first time, the carboxyl groups have been demonstrated as co-reactant sites of C-dots, and we have further discussed the possible ECL mechanism for Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots. A 10-fold increase in ECL intensity was observed when the carboxyl-group concentration on C-dots was increased through hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) oxidation. This work would not only promote the rational design of Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots with high ECL performance for bioanalytical applications but also provide a facile way to investigate the contribution of oxygen-containing functional groups on nanomaterials to their co-reactant behavior.

Carbon fibers were purchased from the Shanghai Tansu Manufactory. HNO3 (65%–68%), HCl (38%), H2SO4 (98%), NaHCO3, NaOH, H2O2 (30%), NaCl, Na2HPO4, PbO2 and NaH2PO4 were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. 1,3-Propane sultone (PS) and deuterium oxide (D2O) were supplied by Energy Chemical and Aladdin, respectively. 1,4-dioxane, Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O, triethylamine (TEA), 2-bromoacetophenone (BrAP), acetonitrile, benzoic anhydride (BA), tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP), tripropylamine (TPrA) and phenylhydrazine (PH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. All the above-mentioned reagents were of analytical grade.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) pictures were captured on a JEM-2100 TEM (JEOL, Japan). Proton magnetic resonance (1H NMR) images and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were recorded with an AVANCE III HD 400 MHz spectrometer (Bruker, Switzerland) and a Multilab 2000 spectrometer (VG, USA), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed by a TENSOR II spectrometer (Bruker, Switzerland). Fluorescence (FL) and chemiluminescence (CL) spectra were detected by a Fluorolog-3 spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out by a UV-2550 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

The cyclic voltammogram (CV) and differential pulse voltammogram (DPV) signals were captured on a CHI617A workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Company, China). The ECL experiments were carried out with an MPI-E multifunctional system (Xi’An Remex Analytical Instrument Co., Ltd., China) held at −800 V. A typical three-electrode cell was employed, including a glassy carbon working electrode, a platinum wire counter electrode, and an Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) reference electrode. To exclude the possible ECL emission interference generated at the counter electrode, an H-shaped electrolytic cell was employed. Unless specifically emphasized, the electrochemical and ECL signals were recorded in 0.2 mol L-1 pH 7.2 phosphate buffer (PBS) by cycling the potential from +0.2 to +1.3 V (vs. Ag/AgCl) with a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. The concentrations of Ru(bpy)3 2+ and various C-dots used in all electrochemical and ECL experiments were 40 μmol L-1 and 0.4 mg mL−1, respectively. ECL spectra were collected using a set of long-pass filters with cutoff wavelengths differing by 20 nm. For example, to collect the ECL spectra of C-dots, 30 uL of C-dots (20 mg mL−1) and 14.3 mg TPrA were added to 1 mL of 0.2 mol L-1 pH 7.2 PBS. The ECL signal of the mixed solution was collected after inserting the long-pass filter. I λh and I λi are the ECL intensities measured when the shorter and longer wavelength filters were inserted, respectively. The difference in intensity ΔI λi (ΔI λi = I λh- I λi) is taken as the ECL intensity at the wavelength λi. The ECL spectra were then obtained by plotting the trend from ΔI λi to λi.

Carbon fibers (0.4 g) were added to an acidic solution (H2SO4:HNO3 = 1:1, v:v). The mixture was heated to 120 °C and refluxed for 2h. The mixture was purified through dialysis. The fractions below 3 kDa were obtained via ultrafiltration through centrifugal filter devices (Millipore) and denoted as C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical passivation of the oxygen-containing functional groups on carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical passivation reactions were carried out with a slight modification to previously described procedures [30,31]. For the passivation of carboxyl groups on C-dots, 5 mL of acetonitrile dispersion of BrAP (0.08 g mL−1) was mixed with 5 mL of water dispersion of C-dots (2 mg mL−1). The mixed solution was stirred for 5h at 25 °C under N2 protection in darkness, then rotary evaporated to remove the solvent and re-dispersed in water. The mixture was washed with CHCl3 to remove unreacted BrAP molecules until the BrAP molecules couldn’t be detected in the organic phase by UV–vis spectra. The sample obtained was dialyzed to give C-dot derivatives, named BrAP-C-dots.

For the passivation of hydroxyl groups on C-dots, 0.5 g of BA was dissolved in 5 mL of acetonitrile, and then 5 mL of C-dots (2 mg mL−1) in water were added. The obtained solution was stirred for 24h at 60 °C under N2 protection and then rotary evaporated to remove the solvent. Further, the same purification procedures as for BrAP-C-dots were carried out. The sample obtained was named BA-C-dots.

To passivate carbonyl groups on C-dots, 0.1 g of pH and 10 µL of HCl (38%) were dissolved in 5 mL of acetonitrile, and then 5 mL of C-dots (2 mg mL−1) in water were added to the above solution. After being stirred for three days at 25 °C under N2 protection, the mixed solution was rotary evaporated to remove the solvent. Then the same purification procedures as for BrAP-C-dots were carried out. The sample obtained was named PH-C-dots.

To passivate hydroxyl and carboxyl groups simultaneously, 5 mg of C-dots were dissolved in 1 mL of water and then mixed with 10 mL of 1,4-dioxane dispersion of PS (0.05 g mL−1) and 1 mL of TEA. The mixture was stirred at 40 °C for one day and then purified through dialysis. The sample obtained was named PS-C-dots.

8 mg of C-dots were dissolved in 10 mL of H2O2 solution. After stirring at 90 °C for 8h, the solution was diluted with ice-cold ultrapure water to cool down to 25 °C and purified through dialysis. The sample obtained was named ox-C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of carbon nanodots and their derivatives
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots were obtained by chemically oxidizing carbon fibers, as we previously reported [32,33], which were mainly covered with carboxyl (COOH), hydroxyl (OH), and carbonyl (CO) groups. BrAP, BA, and PH were employed as deactivating agents with high specificity to react with the COOH, OH, and CO groups of initial C-dots, respectively (Fig. 1A) [34]. The TEM characterization (Fig. 1B-C and Fig. S1) indicated that the as-prepared C-dot derivatives (named BrAP-C-dots, BA-C-dots, and PH-C-dots) were well dispersed and had an average diameter of 2.3 nm (calculated by more than 200 dots), coinciding with the initial C-dots. Furthermore, the high-resolution TEM images (inset in Fig. 1B-C and Fig. S1) revealed that all these C-dots possessed good crystallinity and a well-defined lattice spacing of 2.13 Å, agreeing with the interlayer (100) lattice spacing of graphene [35]. The TEM results demonstrate that neither the average size of C-dots nor their crystalline lattice was changed after the aforementioned three reactions.

Both C-dots and their derivatives showed strong absorption in the UV region (Fig. S2). A well-defined absorption peak was located at 230 nm, ascribed to the π-π* transition. The FL emission wavelength of C-dots and their derivatives did not shift with the excitation wavelength, which may be attributed to the formation of dominant emissive sites on C-dots under intense oxidation conditions [36]. Detailed surface group variations of C-dot derivatives were revealed using XPS, zeta potential characterizations, 13C NMR, and FT-IR spectroscopy. The O 1 s XPS spectra are fitted to three components: CO (531.2 eV), COOH (532.4 eV), and COH (533.5 eV). As demonstrated in Fig. 1D and Table S1, the CO signals at PH-C-dots and the COH signals at BA-C-dots were clearly decreased in the O 1 s XPS spectra with respect to the original C-dots. However, the increased intensities of the CO signals were found in BrAP-C-dots. Furthermore, according to the results of XPS, PH-C-dots showed a significantly higher N content (9.61%) compared to the initial C-dots (1.03%), due to the coupling of pH molecules. As shown in the zeta potential measurements (Fig. 1E), in comparison with other C-dots, BrAP-C-dots had a much lower zeta potential value, suggesting the removal of carboxyl groups by BrAP molecules. As shown in Fig. S3A, the characteristic peaks at ca. 60–70 ppm, 130 ppm, and 173 ppm are attributed to aliphatic carbons, aromatic carbons, and carboxylic/carbonyl groups on C-dots, respectively. For BA-C-dots and BrAP-C-dots, the peaks at ca. 169 ppm belong to the ester group, which is produced by the reactions between hydroxyl groups with BA and carboxyl groups with BrAP. In the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S3B), compared to the initial C-dot, the peak of CO stretching vibration of BA-C-dots and BrAP-C-dots was red-shifted from 1714 cm−1 to 1720 cm−1 and 1723 cm−1, respectively, indicating the formation of ester groups. Furthermore, a small peak at 1800 cm−1 appears in the FT-IR spectrum of the PH-C-dots, which is associated with the CN bonds formed during the reaction with PH. The above results indicated that the three reactions had indeed occurred.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon nanodots or their derivatives as co-reactants for Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL
ce:section.ce:para: After successfully synthesizing C-dots and C-dot derivatives, the ECL-potential profiles of Ru(bpy)3 2+ containing C-dots or their derivatives were investigated to reveal the relationship between the specific oxygen-containing functional groups on C-dots and Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the Ru(bpy)3 2+ solution alone showed a weak ECL emission, which was attributed to the reaction between electro-oxidized Ru(bpy)3 3+ and OH− ion in aqueous solutions [27,37,38]. However, when C-dots or their derivatives were mixed with Ru(bpy)3 2+, significant and stable ECL signals were observed (Fig. S4). The ECL signals gradually increased as the concentration of C-dots or their derivatives increased (Fig. S5). However, once the concentration exceeded 0.4 mg mL−1, side reactions caused the ECL intensity to diminish [4]. Compared to the initial C-dots, the PH-C-dots displayed about the same ECL enhancement effect. However, the BrAP-C-dots and BA-C-dots demonstrated diminished ECL enhancement effects.

Control experiments were conducted to investigate whether the differences came from the ECL emission from the four different C-dots themselves. As depicted in Fig. S6A, the C-dots or C-dot derivatives without Ru(bpy)3 2+ show negligible ECL emission under the identical technical conditions. Additionally, the impacts of the three small molecules (BrAP, BA, and PH) on Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL have also been investigated. According to Fig. S6B, the three small molecules have no effect on Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL. Therefore, the differences in ECL enhancement effect were closely related to the OH and COOH groups on C-dots.

To reveal how the OH and COOH groups on C-dots affect the ECL intensity, the role of C-dots in the mixture was evaluated at first. The ECL spectrum was applied to verify the emitter in the mixture [22]. The ECL spectrum of C-dots had a maximum emission at 530 nm (Fig. S7), close to their FL emission peak (Fig. S2A). The phenomenon was attributed to both FL and ECL of the C-dots deriving from surface states [36]. The mixture of C-dots or their derivatives and Ru(bpy)3 2+ had a maximum emission at 620 nm (Fig. 2B and Fig. S8), which was matched to Ru(bpy)3 2+ rather than C-dots. Therefore, Ru(bpy)3 2+ was the ECL emitter in the mixture. Subsequently, the spectral analysis was used to investigate whether there was energy transfer between Ru(bpy)3 2+ and C-dots or their derivatives. The UV–vis absorption as well as the FL emission spectra of the mixture were simply a combination of the single Ru(bpy)3 2+ and single C-dots or their derivatives (Fig. 2C and Fig. S9). These results reveal that no energy transfer happened from C-dots or their derivatives to Ru(bpy)3 2+ at either their ground-state or excited-state.

The CV experiments were further employed to study the ECL-enhancing mechanism. As displayed in Fig. 2D, one pair of reversible redox peaks were observed at +1.1 V according to the CV profiles for Ru(bpy)3 2+. An enhanced oxidation peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ appeared after adding the C-dots or their derivatives. The enhancement degree by C-dots was identical to PH-C-dots, which was much higher than BA-C-dots and BrAP-C-dots. The phenomenon was consistent with the ECL results. Taking BA-C-dots as an example (Fig. 2E), a linear relationship was observed between the BA-C-dots concentrations and the Ru(bpy)3 2+ oxidation peak intensity, indicating that BA-C-dots were homogeneously oxidized by electro-generated Ru(bpy)3 3+. Furthermore, the CL technique was used to further confirm the redox reaction between C-dots and Ru(bpy)3 3+. As shown in Fig. S10, significant CL emission was observed in the Ru(bpy)3 3+/C-dots system with a peak at 610 nm, indicating C-dots can be oxidized by Ru(bpy)3 3+. These phenomena suggest that C-dots and their derivatives can catalyze the oxidation of Ru(bpy)3 2+, agreeing with the previously reported Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL system with the participation of co-reactants [12]. Thus, the C-dots or their derivatives improve the ECL intensity through the co-reactant pathway rather than energy transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carboxyl groups on carbon nanodots as co-reactant sites
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies demonstrated that the OH groups can act as co-reactant sites on C-dots [22,28]. However, after passivating the OH groups on C-dots, the corresponding C-dot derivative (BA-C-dots) can still increase the ECL intensity. Thus, except for the OH groups, there are other co-reactant sites on C-dots. Based on the discussion above, the COOH and OH groups at C-dots would affect the co-reactant activity of C-dots, thereby affecting the ECL intensity. Therefore, we speculate that the COOH groups at C-dots may act as co-reactant sites. As far as we know, the impact of COOH groups on the co-reactant behavior of C-dots has never been reported before.

To verify our hypothesis, the relationship between pH values and ECL intensity has been investigated. The ECL intensity of the mixture increases with the pH value increasing from 3 to 9 (Fig. 2F). Under the same experiment conditions, benzylic alcohol and oxalic acid, two typical molecular co-reactants, were selected for ECL experiments to understand the co-reactant behavior of the OH and COOH groups at different pH values (inset in Fig. 2F). When the pH is lower than 8, benzyl alcohol is unable to enhance the ECL intensity. However, oxalic acid can enhance the ECL intensity with increasing pH (3 to 9). The phenomenon revealed that only the carboxyl groups on C-dots can generate strong reducing radicals at pH < 8, subsequently reacting with Ru(bpy)3 3+ to yield Ru(bpy)3 2+*. However, once pH > 8, both the OH and COOH groups of C-dots produce reducing radicals, resulting in higher ECL intensity.

In order to gain insight into the co-reactant behavior, PS was chosen for deactivating the OH and COOH groups at C-dots simultaneously (Fig. 3 A). According to Fig. 3B, PS-C-dots were well dispersed, and no noticeable change was observed in particle size or crystalline lattice compared to initial C-dots (Fig. 1B). UV–vis and FL emission spectra of PS-C-dots have been demonstrated in Fig. S11. Compared with initial C-dots, there were no significant changes in the positions of the absorption peak and fluorescence maximum emission peak of PS-C-dots. As shown in Fig. S9D, no energy transfer has happened from Ru(bpy)3 2+ to PS-C-dots at either their ground-state or excited-state. 1H NMR, XPS, and FT-IR characterizations were employed to reveal the changes in surface groups on PS-C-dots. According to 1H NMR results (Fig. 3C), new characteristic absorption peaks appear at 2.2, 3.0, and 4.5 ppm, indicating the successful introduction of the -SO3 - group at PS-C-dots. Besides, compared to initial C-dots, a new element peak (S 2p) for PS-C-dots was observed at 164 eV in the XPS results (Fig. S12), and the S/C was calculated to be 7.3%. Furthermore, as FT-IR results show (Fig. 3D), the absorption peaks of -SO3 – at PS-C-dots were observed at 531, 609, 1046, and 1150 cm−1. Compared with initial C-dots, an obviously stronger peak around 1230 cm−1 was observed in PS-dots due to the formation of ester and the introduction of the sulfonate group. Besides, the stretching vibrational peaks of CO groups were redshifted to 1725 cm−1, indicating the formation of ester groups. Furthermore, the broad peak at ca. 2550 cm−1 attributed to the characteristic absorption of the bonding hydrogen bond disappears with the passivation of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups by PS. These results confirmed that the reaction occurred successfully. Then, the Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL with PS-C-dots has been investigated. Previous studies reported that the introduction of sulfonate groups on co-reactants would enhance co-reactant activity [39]. However, their intensity diminished significantly compared to the original Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots (Fig. 3E). In fact, the Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL barely enhanced after adding PS-C-dots. The above discussion further demonstrated that the COOH and OH groups were co-reactant sites for C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed mechanism for Ru(bpy)3 2+-ECL with carbon nanodots as co-reactants
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. S13, the DPV study of C-dots showed that a peak at + 0.82 V, corresponding to the oxidation of OH groups [28], disappeared after the passivation of OH groups by BA molecules. And the oxidation peak at + 1.06 V disappeared after the passivation of COOH groups by BrAP molecules. However, after the passivation of carbonyl groups by PH molecules, no observable changes in these two peaks were found. On the basis of the discussions above, Scheme 1 suggests the ECL mechanism. The OH and COOH groups at C-dots were electrochemically oxidized and then deprotonated to produce unstable reductive intermediates. The electrochemical oxidization of Ru(bpy)3 2+ produced Ru(bpy)3 3+. Then, the reaction between Ru(bpy)3 3+ and the unstable reductive intermediates yielded the excited-state Ru(bpy)3 2+*, which returned to the ground state and generated the light signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhancing the ECL intensity through surface modification of carbon nanodots
ce:section.ce:para: Further, a surface engineering process was applied to change the oxygen-containing groups at C-dots [40,41]. Ox-C-dots were well dispersed with a mean size of 2.3 nm, demonstrating a well-defined lattice spacing of 2.13 Å (Fig. 4 A). As Fig. 4B shows, the high-resolution C 1 s peaks were fitted into the CC groups at 284.5 eV, the C-OH groups at 286.0 eV, the CO groups at 287.6 eV and the COOH groups at 288.8 eV, respectively [36]. As depicted in Table S2, the content of COOH groups in ox-C-dots increased from 7.5% to 12.4%, whereas the content of CO groups decreased from 14.9% to 9.0%. According to 1H NMR results (Fig. 4C), the α-H peak intensity of the COH and CO groups (3.3–4.3 ppm) decreased in ox-C-dots, whereas the peak intensity of the α-H of the COOH groups (1.8–2.8 ppm) increased, consistent with XPS measurements.

According to Fig. 4D, in the presence of ox-C-dots, the oxidation peak was enhanced and the reduction peak became weaker in the CV profile of Ru(bpy)3 2+. The changes in the redox peak of Ru(bpy)3 2+ were derived from Ru(bpy)3 3+ reacting with the ox-C-dots, matching the discussions above. As Fig. 4E shows, the ECL intensity increased a lot after treating the C-dots with H2O2. Compared with the original Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots system, the Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the presence of ox-C-dots had a 10 times higher ECL intensity. To our knowledge, the enhancement method has never been reported before. However, the ECL intensity of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots system still has much room for improvement. Further increasing the concentration of COOH and OH groups on C-dots through chemical reactions or covalently linking the C-dots with Ru(bpy)3 2+ to reduce the energy loss [27] would improve the ECL intensity of the Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots system to meet high-sensitivity detection. Besides, in contrast to TPrA (Fig. S14), the ECL intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+ was relatively constant (RSD = 2.0%) in the presence of ox-C-dots for 21 cycles (Fig. 4F) and had excellent 1-week repeatability. The CCK8 assays have been conducted to investigate the cytotoxicity of the ox-C-dots on mouse mammary tumor cells (4 T1) and canine kidney cells (MDCK) cells. Fig. S15 shows that both the 4 T1 and MDCK cells maintained good viabilities (over 95%) even after 24 h of incubation with 500 μg mL−1 ox-C-dots, indicating the negligible cytotoxicity of our synthesized ox-C-dots. Considering the low toxicity of C-dots and the abundant functional groups on C-dots that can easily be modified with biomolecules, the Ru(bpy)3 2+/C-dots ECL system has great potential for bioanalysis and cell imaging.

In summary, to study co-reactant sites of C-dots, the carboxyl groups (COOH), hydroxyl groups (OH), and carbonyl groups (CO) were selectively deactivated, respectively. Employing UV–vis, FL, CV, and ECL experiments, the OH and COOH groups were revealed to affect the co-reactant behavior of C-dots. The COOH groups on C-dots were found to be the co-reactant sites for the first time. By increasing the content of COOH groups through H2O2 oxidation, the ECL intensity could increase10-fold. The clarification of the different functions of the three typical oxygen-containing functional groups on C-dots would enhance our understanding of C-dots as co-reactants. Accordingly, increasing the content of –OH and –COOH groups on C-dots would be beneficial for improving ECL efficiency.

Ying Peng: Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Zhi-Gang Wang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Bao-Ping Qi: Methodology, Formal analysis. Cui Liu: Formal analysis. Bo Tang: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Zhi-Ling Zhang: Formal analysis. Shu-Lin Liu: Writing – review & editing. Dai-Wen Pang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22293030, 22293032), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFA0210100), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities of China (63211023), and the Haihe Laboratory of Sustainable Chemical Transformations.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2023.09.122.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
